intro:
Eternal life (Greek zoe aionios, "the life of the age [to come]") is, in Scripture, far more than unending duration. It is a quality of life as much as a quantity — the very life of God himself, shared with redeemed humanity, the life proper to the coming age breaking into the present. To possess it is not merely to live forever but to participate in the deathless, blessed life of the Creator, in unbroken fellowship with him. Hence Jesus can define it personally: "this is eternal life, that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ whom you have sent" (John 17:3).

The Old Testament approaches the theme by degrees. In Eden stands the tree of life, access to which is lost in the Fall and guarded by the cherubim (Genesis 3:22–24) — a sign that unending life belongs to God to give. The Psalms voice a confidence that God's presence is "life," that he will not abandon his faithful to the grave, and that there is "fullness of joy" at his right hand forever (Psalm 16:11; Psalm 73:24–26). The hope becomes explicit in the prophets: Isaiah foresees death itself swallowed up (Isaiah 25:8), and Daniel speaks plainly of resurrection — "many of those who sleep in the dust of the earth shall awake, some to everlasting life" (Daniel 12:2).

In the New Testament eternal life comes into full light, supremely in John's Gospel and letters. Jesus is the bread of life and the living water springing up "to eternal life" (John 4:14; John 6:35); he is the resurrection and the life (John 11:25–26). Strikingly, this life is a present possession: whoever believes "has eternal life" now and "has passed from death to life" (John 5:24; 1 John 5:11–13), even as it reaches its fullness in the resurrection at the last day (John 6:40). In the Synoptics it is what the rich ruler seeks and what disciples inherit in "the age to come" (Mark 10:17, 30; Matthew 25:46), and Jesus' great promise is that those who believe "should not perish but have eternal life" (John 3:16; John 10:28).

Paul sets it in sharp relief against sin and death: "the wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord" (Romans 6:23). It is therefore wholly of grace — never earned — secured by union with the risen Christ and sealed by the Spirit as an inheritance (Titus 3:7), to be consummated in the redemption of the body when "this mortal" puts on immortality (1 Corinthians 15:53–54). Scripture's storyline closes where it began but gloriously restored: the tree of life stands again in the New Jerusalem, the curse undone, and "death shall be no more" as God dwells forever with his people (Revelation 21:3–4; Revelation 22:1–2). Eternal life is thus both gift and goal — the present pledge and the everlasting enjoyment of God, who alone has life in himself and gives it freely to all who come to his Son.
